conclusion: Renaissance Technologies — especially the Medallion fund — is the best-performing investment firm in history: ~39% net annualized over decades with no losing years after 1990, closed to outsiders since 1993, capped near ~$10B because edge decays with size. Jim Simons (codebreaker, Stony Brook builder) and mathematicians (Berlekamp, Ax, Mercer) found faint signals — right 50.75% of the time at huge frequency — not macro narratives. Medallion spun out of Axcom's hidden quant success while RenTech ran VC; Lenny Baum's bond blowup showed blow-up risk even here. Capacity limits forced 36–44% carry, then expelling external LPs in 2003. RIEF/RAM institutional products market lower-Sharpe 'not Medallion' beta. Lesson: edges are small, secret, and size-constrained — the opposite of Berkshire's public compounder model.
background: Season 14 Episode 3 (~188 minutes) follows Jim Simons from IDA codebreaking and Stony Brook (Rockefeller's $100M push) through Lenny Baum's ~40% bond blowup, Axcom's hidden quant success, Medallion's 1988 launch, 1990+ undefeated streak (~39% net annualized), 1993 fund closure, 2003 LP expulsion at ~$5B, and ~$150–200B cash distributed over 35 years. Covers Mercer political controversy and institutional RIEF products that explicitly disclaim Medallion performance.
important_facts: Medallion fund: ~39% net annualized over ~30 years after 1990 with zero losing years; 2000 gross +128%, net +98.5% while tech crashed — high volatility favors the strategy.
Fund closed to new outside capital 1993; outside LPs expelled by 2003 as AUM neared ~$5B capacity ceiling — Peter Brown and Bob Mercer later raised cap to ~$10B without degrading Sharpe.
Bob Mercer: 'We are right 50.75% of the time' — edge is tiny win rate times enormous trade count, not directional conviction. Five-minute price predictions beat three-year guesses.
Fees escalated to 5% management (~$800K/year infrastructure) plus 36–44% carry — justified because early investors were employees; 2001 carry rose to 36%, 2002 to 44%.
Estimated ~$60B+ performance fees to owners; ~$150–200B total cash distributed from Medallion over 35 years. 2007: +136% gross; 2008: +152% gross during financial crisis. 2020: +149% gross, +76% net.

mental_model.name: Micro-Edge at Scale Frequency
mental_model.components: Micro-edge at scale frequency: Mercer 50.75% accuracy over millions of daily trades. Five-minute predictions beat three-year guesses. Medallion ~39% net, zero losing years post-1990; 2000 +98.5% net while tech crashed; 2008 +152% gross in financial crisis. Capacity ~$5–10B before slippage; closed 1993, expelled outsiders 2003. Carry 36–44% on employee capital. ~$150–200B distributed over 35 years. 25 years IDA to working fund; Baum ~40% bond loss nearly killed firm. RIEF ~8.6% in 2023 — explicitly not Medallion.
mental_model.application: Medallion inaccessible — study capacity limits on any alpha strategy. RIEF offers index-like returns with RenTech brand. Risk management beat prediction after Baum blowup. Sharpe ratio matters more than headline return.

competitive_advantage: Medallion's moat is proprietary data pipeline, execution infrastructure, and academic culture — not a single formula. ~39% net annualized over ~30 years after 1990 with zero losing years; 2000 delivered +128% gross/+98.5% net while tech crashed; 2007 +136% gross; 2008 +152% gross during financial crisis; 2020 +149% gross/+76% net. Bob Mercer's 50.75% accuracy at enormous frequency compounds via leverage — five-minute predictions beat three-year guesses. Jim Simons built Stony Brook with Rockefeller's $100M math push; recruited Berlekamp, Ax, Mercer, Brown. Early RenTech was 50% VC / 50% currency trading until Lenny Baum's ~40% bond drawdown triggered exit. Medallion closed to new LPs 1993 at confidence returns exceed inflows; expelled outsiders 2003 at ~$5B AUM with 36–44% carry. Capacity discipline: slippage when models want $5M in thin markets — AUM grew purely from gains ($276M in 1994 to $3.8B by 2000). ~$60B+ performance fees; ~$150–200B total cash distributed over 35 years. 5% management fee backed into ~$800K infrastructure need. Weaknesses: Mercer political controversy; irreplicable for outsiders. RIEF returned ~8.6% through Nov 2023 — explicitly 'not the Medallion fund.' Versus Berkshire: RenTech extracts micro-inefficiencies that die if published or scaled. Early Medallion 5% management fee vs 3% top VC firms — backed into $800K Straus infrastructure costs. 1992–93 gross 47–54% before 1993 closure. Performance fees $7M, $13M, $52M in early 1990s scaling partner cash flow. Peter Brown and Bob Mercer co-CEOs from 2010 took portfolio to $10B from $5B without Sharpe degradation. Jim retired end 2009 after historic 2007–08 crisis gains. First Round Fund I 50X on $125M cited as venture comparison. Initial RenTech capital under $4M with Lenny Baum. Simons chain-smoked ~75 years. Apologist nuance: Buffett could be other side of trade at 50-year horizon while Medallion profits at 1.5-day hold. High volatility regimes 2007 2008 2020 favor models.  Mercer political controversy noted. Institutional products hold months not one day. Campus culture reduces leakage versus Wall Street turnover. Ben: 6X in one year on carry sounds great but $60B cumulative dividends to owners is the real wealth story. Episode covers opposite of Berkshire transparent compounder model.  David: 2003 expulsion of outside LPs coincided with ~$5B Medallion AUM capacity ceiling in thin markets before equities expansion.

key_insights.question: How can tiny accuracy beat Buffett?
key_insights.answer: Mercer: correct slightly more than half the time at huge frequency with leverage and risk controls compounds to ~39% net for decades. 1990 first full winning year: +77.8% gross, +55% net. Fundamental investors need deep stories; RenTech needs statistical significance on millions of trades. Five-minute horizons stay predictable; three-year horizons revert to noise. 1990: +77.8% gross, +55% net first full winning year; 1991: +54.3% gross, +39.4% net. Ax burnout led Berlekamp to buy out Ax at ~$27M AUM inflection.

key_insights.question: Why close Medallion at ~$10B?
key_insights.answer: Slippage: models want size markets cannot fill — especially pre-equities in currencies/commodities. At ~$5B AUM in 2003, external LPs expelled; carry already 36–44%. AUM grew purely from gains ($276M in 1994 → $3.8B by 2000) not new investors. Buffett's 'too big' problem shares logic but opposite solution. 2000: fund grew $1.9B to $3.8B purely from gains while tech bubble burst. 1994 +93% gross on $276M AUM scaling purely via reinvested gains.

key_insights.question: Was success inevitable?
key_insights.answer: Lenny Baum's bond losses (~40%) expelled partner; early Medallion stumbled until Berlekamp bought Ax out at ~$27M AUM. 25 years from IDA papers to working fund. RenTech started 50% VC / 50% currency trading — Axcom quant success hid inside VC-era firm. Berlekamp bought out Ax at ~$27M AUM after early Medallion stumble. External LPs could not be incentivized out until carry hit 44%.

key_insights.question: Can I buy Medallion exposure?
key_insights.answer: No — outside LPs expelled 2003. RIEF returned ~8.6% through Nov 2023, index-like; down 10.4% first 4 months of 2020 vs broader market. Documents disclaim Medallion returns; longer hold periods, lower Sharpe by design. RenTech monetizes reputation while guarding crown jewel. RIEF down 10.4% first 4 months of 2020 — mirror of broader market, unlike Medallion. Jim Simons philanthropy accelerated post-retirement 2009.

key_insights.view: Culture is campus, not Wall Street.
key_insights.question: Why Long Island PhDs?
key_insights.answer: Jim built Stony Brook with Rockefeller's $100M math push; recruited Berlekamp, Ax, Mercer, Brown. 5% management fee backed into ~$800K infrastructure need. Secrecy, code ownership, extreme carry on closed capital — no AUM marketing treadmill. ~$150–200B cash distributed to principals over 35 years. Initial capital under $4M; 25 years from IDA papers to Medallion working. Episode frames RenTech opposite Berkshire public compounder transparency model.

top_investment_implications.thesis: Medallion inaccessible. RIEF and quant peers offer different risk/return — capacity-limited alpha case study, not buyable ticker.

golden_quotes: "We are right 50.75% of the time" — Bob Mercer on Medallion edge.
"$150–$200 billion out of Medallion over 35 years" — total cash distributed.
"This is not the Medallion fund" — institutional product disclaimer.
